meta.scene_summary: We are busy optimizing compilers and libraries while the hardware leaks our secrets and the infrastructure poisons the well.

cooking.headline: Zig is officially decluttering the compiler one feature at a time
cooking.summary: Zig is moving package management out of the compiler, finally admitting that building code and managing dependencies are two different chores. It's the software engineering equivalent of moving your laundry out of the kitchen so you can actually cook dinner.
cooking.plain_english: The developers of the Zig programming language have separated the package management tools from the main compiler to simplify the software's architecture.

cooking.headline: Shadcn/UI swaps out its library foundation like a developer changing frameworks
cooking.summary: Shadcn/UI is ditching Radix for Base UI, which is basically the coding version of breaking up with your partner to date their cooler, more flexible sibling. It's a great move for stability, provided you don't mind updating every single component in your codebase this weekend.
cooking.plain_english: The Shadcn/UI component library has changed its underlying framework from Radix UI to Base UI to improve its functionality.

cooking.headline: Cold boot attacks are back because your RAM has no short-term memory
cooking.summary: This tool lets you rip data straight from RAM by rebooting a machine, proving that physical access is still the ultimate admin password. It is basically the digital equivalent of stealing someone's wallet while they're still wearing the pants.
cooking.plain_english: This software allows a user to extract information from a computer's memory by restarting the machine without clearing the data.

cooking.headline: A library for $150 and a dream: The AI-written release is here
cooking.summary: Simon Willison just outsourced a major software update to an AI for less than the cost of a fancy office chair. It turns out the future of programming is just being the human project manager for an intern that never sleeps.
cooking.plain_english: A developer released a significant software update that was primarily written by an artificial intelligence model for a very low cost.

cooked.headline: Windows telemetry is now basically a digital stalker with admin rights
cooked.summary: Microsoft is collecting your full browsing and gaming history under the guise of telemetry, because apparently your OS needs to know exactly how many hours you spent doomscrolling. It's like having a landlord who insists on reading your diary just to make sure the lightbulbs are working.
cooked.plain_english: Microsoft is gathering detailed information about users' internet activity and gaming habits through a tracking feature built into the Windows operating system.

cooked.headline: YouTube's security forgot that unlisted doesn't mean invisible
cooked.summary: YouTube managed to leak private creator videos by treating 'unlisted' like a secret handshake rather than an actual permission check. It turns out that security through obscurity is still just a fancy way of saying you didn't finish the group project.
cooked.plain_english: A security vulnerability allowed unauthorized users to access and view private or unlisted videos on the YouTube platform.

cooked.headline: Junior developers are currently being replaced by a script that can't even center a div.
cooked.summary: We've reached a point where companies are cutting entry-level roles because they think AI can handle the grunt work. It turns out that when you stop hiring juniors, you eventually stop having seniors who know how the legacy code actually works.
cooked.plain_english: Companies are hiring fewer entry-level software developers because they are relying more on artificial intelligence tools to write code.

cooked.headline: GPT-5.5 is currently having a mid-life crisis and forgetting how to code.
cooked.summary: The model is suffering from a logic loop where it keeps overthinking its own output until the code turns into gibberish. It's basically the equivalent of a student who studied so hard for the exam that they talked themselves into failing it.
cooked.plain_english: The latest version of OpenAI's coding assistant is experiencing technical issues where its reasoning process is causing it to produce lower-quality code.

cooked.headline: Meta's data center is literally poisoning the local water supply
cooked.summary: Meta spent billions building AI infrastructure only to realize they forgot how to handle basic plumbing. It turns out that 'cloud computing' shouldn't involve turning the town's water supply into a toxic science experiment.
cooked.plain_english: Cheyenne officials have stopped Meta from discharging water from its data center because the company's cooling system contaminated the local water reuse network.

cooked.headline: Verizon is bricking kids' watches to force an upgrade
cooked.summary: Verizon is pushing a network change that turns perfectly functional Gizmo watches into expensive paperweights just to upsell parents on new hardware. It is the classic corporate strategy of 'planned obsolescence' disguised as a network improvement.
cooked.plain_english: Verizon is ending support for older Gizmo watch models, which will cause them to stop working and force customers to buy newer devices.
